questions.question: Two fair coins are tossed independently. X is a random variable that takes a value of 1 if both tosses are heads and 0 otherwise. Y is a random variable that takes a value of 1 if at least one of the tosses is heads and 0 otherwise. The value of the covariance of X and Y is __________ (rounded off to three decimal places).

understand.plain: Two fair coins are flipped. X equals 1 only when both land heads (probability 1/4), and Y equals 1 when at least one lands heads (probability 3/4). Covariance measures how much two random variables change together. Here, whenever X is 1 (both heads) Y must also be 1, which creates a positive dependency that we quantify as Cov(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y].

keywords.explain: Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]; positive means both tend to be large together, zero means uncorrelated.
keywords.example: If X=1 forces Y=1, then E[XY] > E[X]E[Y], giving positive covariance.

keywords.explain: A function mapping each outcome of a probability experiment to a real number.
keywords.example: X = 1 when both coins are H, else X = 0.

keywords.explain: P(X=a, Y=b) = probability that X equals a AND Y equals b simultaneously.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='420' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Sample Space (2 fair coins)</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='65' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>HH</text><text x='65' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X=1, Y=1</text><rect x='120' y='45' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='165' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>HT</text><text x='165' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X=0, Y=1</text><rect x='220' y='45' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='265' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>TH</text><text x='265' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X=0, Y=1</text><rect x='320' y='45' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='365' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>TT</text><text x='365' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X=0, Y=0</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub ink'>E[X] = 1/4   E[Y] = 3/4   E[XY] = 1/4</text><text x='20' y='153' class='sub ink'>Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = 1/4 - (1/4)(3/4)</text><text x='20' y='176' class='lbl ok'>= 4/16 - 3/16 = 1/16 = 0.0625</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table of all four coin-toss outcomes with X and Y values shown, then covariance formula applied step by step.

given.aim: Find Cov(X, Y) where X = 1 iff both coins land heads, Y = 1 iff at least one coin lands heads.

terms.meaning: Indicator random variable: X = 1 if outcome is HH, else X = 0.
Indicator random variable: Y = 1 if at least one head, else Y = 0.
Expected value of the product XY.
terms.example: XY = 1 only when both X=1 and Y=1, which happens only at HH (prob 1/4).
terms.connects: Central to the covariance formula Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y].

given.plan: List all four equally likely outcomes. Compute E[X], E[Y], and E[XY] directly. Apply the covariance formula.

steps.title: Identify outcome probabilities
steps.apply: 4 equally likely outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT each with probability 1/4. X=1 only at HH. Y=1 at HH, HT, TH.
steps.note: Since X=1 implies both coins are H, it automatically implies at least one head, so Y=1 whenever X=1.

steps.title: Compute E[X] and E[Y]
steps.apply: E[X] = P(HH) = 1/4. E[Y] = P(HH or HT or TH) = 3/4.
steps.note: For indicator (Bernoulli) variables the expectation equals the probability of the event.

steps.title: Compute E[XY]
steps.formula_raw: E[XY] = sum over outcomes of X*Y * P(outcome)
steps.apply: XY = 1 only at HH (X=1, Y=1). At all other outcomes XY = 0. So E[XY] = 1*(1/4) = 1/4.
steps.note: At HT and TH, X=0, so XY=0. At TT, both X=0 and Y=0.

steps.title: Apply covariance formula
steps.note: Rounded to three decimal places: 0.062 or 0.063 depending on rounding convention; exact value is 0.0625.

solution.result: Cov(X, Y) = 1/16 = 0.0625 (approximately 0.062 to three decimal places)

questions.formula_note: Only the basic probability and expectation definitions are needed; covariance formula Cov = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] is applied directly.

questions.question: A quadratic polynomial (z - a)(z - b) over complex numbers is said to be square invariant if (z - a)(z - b) = (z - a^2)(z - b^2). Suppose from the set of all square invariant quadratic polynomials we choose one at random. The probability that the roots of the chosen polynomial are equal is __________ (rounded off to one decimal place).

understand.plain: A square invariant polynomial has the property that its root set {a, b} is unchanged when every root is squared: {a^2, b^2} = {a, b}. We need to find all such polynomials, then count how many have equal roots (a = b). The probability is (count with equal roots) divided by (total count).

keywords.explain: A polynomial is square invariant when squaring each root gives back the same set of roots.
keywords.example: (z-1)^2 is square invariant because squaring root 1 gives 1 again.

keywords.explain: Complex numbers omega satisfying omega^n = 1; primitive cube roots satisfy omega^3 = 1, omega != 1.
keywords.example: omega = e^(2*pi*i/3) satisfies omega^2 = omega^(-1), and squaring maps between omega and omega^2.

keywords.explain: When a = b, the polynomial is a perfect square (z - a)^2.
keywords.example: z^2 and (z-1)^2 both have equal (repeated) roots.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='420' height='210' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='26' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>All Square-Invariant Polynomials</text><rect x='10' y='38' width='190' height='52' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='105' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>z^2   (roots: 0, 0)</text><text x='105' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Equal roots</text><rect x='215' y='38' width='195' height='52' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='312' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(z-1)^2  (roots: 1, 1)</text><text x='312' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Equal roots</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='190' height='52' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='105' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>z(z-1)  (roots: 0, 1)</text><text x='105' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Distinct roots</text><rect x='215' y='100' width='195' height='52' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='312' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(z-w)(z-w^2)  cube roots</text><text x='312' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Distinct roots</text><text x='210' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P(equal roots) = 2/4 = 0.5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grid showing all four square-invariant polynomials, with two having equal roots (highlighted) and two having distinct roots.

given.aim: Find all square invariant quadratic polynomials (z-a)(z-b), then compute P(roots are equal).

terms.meaning: {a, b} = {a^2, b^2} as sets of roots.
terms.example: For a=0: 0^2 = 0, so 0 maps to itself under squaring.
terms.connects: Two cases: (A) each root maps to itself, or (B) roots swap under squaring.

terms.meaning: a = a^2 and b = b^2, giving a, b in {0, 1}.
terms.example: Yields polynomials with roots from {0, 1}: both 0, both 1, or one each.

terms.meaning: a = b^2 and b = a^2, meaning squaring permutes the roots.
terms.example: omega = e^(2*pi*i/3): omega^2 = omega^(-1) and (omega^2)^2 = omega^4 = omega.
terms.connects: Gives new pair (omega, omega^2) not from Case A.

given.plan: Enumerate all root-set solutions to {a,b} = {a^2, b^2}. Count distinct polynomials. Count those with equal roots. Divide.

steps.title: Case A — each root is a fixed point of squaring
steps.apply: Both a and b independently in {0,1}. Distinct polynomials: roots (0,0) giving z^2; roots (0,1) giving z(z-1); roots (1,1) giving (z-1)^2.

steps.title: Case B — roots swap under squaring
steps.formula_raw: a = b^2, b = a^2 => a^4 = a => a in {0, 1, omega, omega^2}
steps.apply: a=0 or a=1 lead to pairs already counted. New solution: a = omega, b = omega^2 where omega = e^(2*pi*i/3), a primitive cube root of unity. Polynomial: (z-omega)(z-omega^2) = z^2 + z + 1.
steps.note: omega^2 squared = omega^4 = omega^3 * omega = 1 * omega = omega, confirming the swap.

steps.title: List all 4 distinct square invariant polynomials
steps.apply: 1) z^2 (equal roots: 0,0)  2) z(z-1) (distinct roots: 0,1)  3) (z-1)^2 (equal roots: 1,1)  4) z^2+z+1 (distinct roots: omega, omega^2).
steps.note: Choosing uniformly means each polynomial has probability 1/4.

steps.title: Compute probability of equal roots
steps.apply: Polynomials with equal roots: z^2 and (z-1)^2. Count = 2. P(equal roots) = 2/4 = 0.5.

questions.formula_note: The key formula is just classical probability; the main work is enumerating all square-invariant polynomials by solving a = a^2 and the swap equations.

questions.question: For a given biased coin, the probability that the outcome of a toss is a head is 0.6. This coin is tossed 1,000 times. Let X denote the random variable whose value is the number of times that head appeared in these tosses. The standard deviation of X (rounded to 2 decimal places) is __________.

understand.plain: The number of heads in 1000 independent coin tosses with p = 0.6 follows a Binomial distribution. For a Binomial(n, p) random variable, the standard deviation is sqrt(n * p * (1-p)). We substitute n = 1000, p = 0.6, and compute.

keywords.explain: Models the count of successes in n independent trials each with success probability p.
keywords.example: Number of heads in 1000 tosses with P(head) = 0.6 is Binomial(1000, 0.6).

keywords.explain: Square root of the variance; measures how spread the distribution is around its mean.
keywords.example: For Binomial(n,p), Var = n*p*(1-p) so SD = sqrt(n*p*(1-p)).

keywords.explain: Var(X) = n * p * (1 - p), derived from the sum of independent Bernoulli variances.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a direct formula application.

given.aim: Compute the standard deviation of X ~ Binomial(1000, 0.6).

terms.example: Each toss is independent with the same probability.
terms.connects: Enters the variance formula as the multiplier.

terms.meaning: Probability of success (head) on each toss.
terms.example: Biased coin gives head 60% of the time.
terms.connects: p and (1-p)=0.4 enter the variance formula.

terms.example: Sum of variances of 1000 independent Bernoulli(0.6) variables.

given.plan: Identify the Binomial parameters, apply Var = n*p*(1-p), then take the square root.

steps.title: Identify distribution
steps.apply: X counts heads in 1000 independent tosses; each toss has P(head) = 0.6. So X ~ Binomial(1000, 0.6).
steps.note: Independence and identical probability per toss justify the Binomial model.

steps.title: Compute standard deviation
steps.note: Rounded to 2 decimal places: 15.49. This is in the range 15.00:16.00 given by the answer key.

solution.result: Standard deviation of X = sqrt(240) = 15.49 (rounded to 2 decimal places)

questions.formula_note: The Binomial variance formula Var = n*p*(1-p) is the only formula needed; standard deviation is its square root.

questions.question:
Let Z and W be random variables, not necessarily independent, that take real values in the interval [0, 1]. Let mu_Z = E[Z] and mu_W = E[W] be the mean values of Z and W respectively. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Z <= mu_W
B. Z <= mu_Z
C. E[ZW] >= mu_Z * mu_W
D. E[ZW] <= (mu_Z + mu_W) / 2

understand.plain: Since Z and W are both bounded in [0,1], we can bound their product ZW. Because W is at most 1 we have ZW <= Z, and because Z is at most 1 we have ZW <= W. Combining: ZW <= min(Z, W) <= (Z + W)/2 (AM-GM style). Taking expectations gives E[ZW] <= (E[Z] + E[W])/2 = (mu_Z + mu_W)/2, which is statement D.

keywords.explain: A variable that always stays within a fixed interval, e.g. Z in [0,1] means 0 <= Z <= 1 always.
keywords.example: Z in [0,1] means ZW <= Z since W <= 1.

keywords.explain: For non-negative a, b: min(a,b) <= (a+b)/2 (the minimum is at most the average).
keywords.example: min(Z, W) <= (Z + W)/2 for Z, W in [0,1].

keywords.explain: If X <= Y for all outcomes then E[X] <= E[Y].
keywords.example: Since ZW <= (Z+W)/2 pointwise, E[ZW] <= E[(Z+W)/2] = (mu_Z + mu_W)/2.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure inequality argument.

given.aim: Determine which statement about E[ZW] for Z, W in [0,1] is always true.

terms.meaning: Both random variables are always in the unit interval regardless of the outcome.
terms.example: 0 <= Z <= 1 and 0 <= W <= 1 for every sample point.
terms.connects: Key constraint: since W <= 1, we get ZW <= Z*1 = Z.

terms.example: mu_Z = integral of z * f_Z(z) dz.
terms.connects: Taking expectation of ZW <= (Z+W)/2 gives E[ZW] <= (mu_Z + mu_W)/2.

terms.meaning: A and B compare a random variable to a fixed constant; D is deterministic inequality.
terms.example: Z > mu_Z is possible (Z can exceed its own mean), so A and B are not always true. C fails when Z and W are negatively correlated.
terms.connects: Only D holds universally by pointwise inequality.

given.plan: Use the pointwise bound ZW <= min(Z,W) <= (Z+W)/2 (since both are in [0,1]), then take expectations.

steps.title: Establish pointwise upper bound on ZW
steps.apply: Since 0 <= W <= 1 and 0 <= Z <= 1: ZW <= Z and ZW <= W. Therefore ZW <= min(Z, W).
steps.note: This holds for every sample point, not just on average.

steps.title: Apply AM bound: min <= average
steps.note: The minimum of two numbers never exceeds their average.

steps.title: Take expectations
steps.formula_raw: E[X] <= E[Y] if X <= Y pointwise
steps.apply: E[ZW] <= E[(Z+W)/2] = (E[Z] + E[W])/2 = (mu_Z + mu_W)/2. Statement D is proven.
steps.note: No independence assumption is needed; the inequality is purely pointwise.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: A and B are false: a random variable can exceed its mean (take Z = 1, mu_Z = 0.5). C is false: if Z and W are independent uniform on [0,1], E[ZW] = 1/4 = mu_Z * mu_W (equality), but with negative dependence E[ZW] < mu_Z * mu_W.
steps.note: Option D is the only statement true for ALL possible Z, W in [0,1] regardless of dependence.

solution.result: D. E[ZW] <= (mu_Z + mu_W) / 2 is always true for Z, W in [0,1].

questions.formula_note: The proof uses only monotonicity of expectation and the pointwise inequality ZW <= (Z+W)/2 for variables in [0,1]; no special distribution formula is needed.

questions.question: Two friends agree to meet at a park with the following conditions. Each will reach the park between 4:00 pm and 5:00 pm and will see if the other has already arrived. If not, they will wait for 10 minutes or the end of the hour whichever is earlier and leave. What is the probability that the two will NOT meet?

understand.plain: Each friend arrives uniformly at random in the 60-minute window [0, 60]. They meet if and only if |X - Y| <= 10, where X and Y are their arrival times in minutes after 4:00 pm. The probability they do NOT meet is P(|X - Y| > 10). Geometrically this is the fraction of the unit square where |x - y| > 10.

keywords.explain: Arrival time is equally likely to be any value in [0, 60], so the pair (X, Y) is uniform over a 60x60 square.
For two-dimensional uniform distributions, probability equals the ratio of favorable area to total area.
keywords.example: P(|X-Y| <= 10) = area of band |x-y| <= 10 in [0,60]^2 divided by 60^2.

keywords.explain: The region |x - y| > 10 consists of two triangles in the 60x60 square: one where x - y > 10 and one where y - x > 10.
keywords.example: Each triangle has base and height equal to 60-10 = 50, so area = (1/2)*50*50 = 1250.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='20' width='240' height='240' rx='4' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' opacity='0.25' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='30,20 30,228 238,20' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' opacity='0.55'/><polygon points='270,62 270,260 62,260' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='30' y1='20' x2='270' y2='260' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='30' y1='62' x2='230' y2='260' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='70' y1='20' x2='270' y2='220' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='150' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>MEET</text><text x='90' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>NO MEET</text><text x='210' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>NO MEET</text><text x='20' y='20' class='sub ink' transform='rotate(-90,20,150)'>Y (mins)</text><text x='150' y='282' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>X (mins)</text><text x='28' y='22' class='sub mut'>60</text><text x='28' y='262' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='268' y='275' class='sub mut'>60</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 60x60 square representing joint arrival times; green band near the diagonal where |X-Y| <= 10 is the meeting region; two red triangles in the corners are the non-meeting regions.

given.aim: Find the probability that the two friends do NOT meet, i.e., P(|X - Y| > 10) where X, Y ~ Uniform[0, 60].

terms.meaning: Arrival times independently and uniformly distributed over the 60-minute window.
terms.example: The pair (X, Y) is uniform over the square [0,60] x [0,60].

terms.meaning: One friend arrives more than 10 minutes after the other, so the first person has already left.
terms.example: If X = 5 and Y = 20, then Y - X = 15 > 10, so they do NOT meet.
terms.connects: Geometrically this is two triangles, each with legs of length 50.

terms.meaning: Each right triangle has legs of length 60 - 10 = 50.
terms.connects: Two such triangles give total non-meeting area = 2500.

given.plan: Set up the geometric model on [0,60]^2. Find the total area of the non-meeting region (where |X-Y| > 10). Divide by 3600.

steps.title: Set up the sample space
steps.apply: (X, Y) is uniform over [0,60] x [0,60]. Total area = 3600.
steps.note: Each point in the square is equally likely since both arrivals are uniform and independent.

steps.title: Identify non-meeting region
steps.formula_raw: Non-meeting: |X - Y| > 10, i.e., X - Y > 10 or Y - X > 10
steps.apply: Two triangular regions: (1) X - Y > 10: triangle with vertices (10,0), (60,0), (60,50). (2) Y - X > 10: triangle with vertices (0,10), (0,60), (50,60).
steps.note: Each triangle has two legs of length 50.

steps.title: Compute area of non-meeting region
steps.formula_raw: Area of right triangle = (1/2) * base * height
steps.apply: Each triangle: area = (1/2) * 50 * 50 = 1250. Total non-meeting area = 2 * 1250 = 2500.
steps.note: The two triangles are symmetric across the main diagonal.

steps.title: Compute probability they do NOT meet
steps.formula_raw: P(not meet) = non-meeting area / total area
steps.note: Rounded to two decimal places: 0.69. This matches the answer range 0.69:0.70.

questions.formula_note: Geometric probability on a uniform square; the non-meeting region is two right triangles each with legs 50, giving area 2500 out of total 3600.
